Several guidelines have proposed specific rehabilitation programs to treat symptoms/sequelae in patients with post-acute COVID syndrome (PACS). Only a few studies show that rehabilitation reduce disability after COVID-19.

To describe the response to a personalized rehabilitation program in terms of exercise tolerance, muscle function, dyspnea, quality of life, and frailty state in a cohort of patients with PACS.

Observational prospective cohort study of community-dwelling patients. The rehabilitation program included three modalities (outpatient, home-based, and self-administered), optionally supplemented with respiratory muscle training. Study variables: 6-minute walking distance (6MWD), inspiratory muscle strength (PImax), dyspnea, muscle strength (estimated with the Medical Research Council scale and handgrip), health-related quality of life (HRQoL), and frailty estimated with the Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB) and at 3- and 6-months following baseline.

Of 178 patients with PACS, 101 (56.7%) were enrolled in a rehabilitation program: 41.6% home-based, 27.7% outpatient, and 24.8% self-administered. Inspiratory muscle training (IMT) was provided to 56 patients, with 50 included in the rehabilitation program and 6 (5.9%) receiving IMT alone. Significant improvements were observed at 3 months in mMRC strength (p=0.001), SPPB (p<0.0001), handgrip strength (p=0.017), mMRC dyspnea, PImax (p<0.0001), and 6-MWD (p=0.008). Improvements persisted at 6 months for mMRC strength (p=0.048), SPPB (p=0.002), PImax (p<0.0001), and 6MWD (p=0.048). HRQoL mental and physical component scores improved only at 3 months.

Personalized rehabilitation for patients with PACS improved muscle parameters, dyspnoea, and exercise capacity, but not HRQoL, up to 6 months. Improvements in respiratory muscle strength were associated with reductions in dyspnoea and increased exercise capacity.

Varias guías han propuesto programas de rehabilitación específicos y multidimensionales para tratar síntomas y secuelas en pacientes con síndrome COVID postagudo (PACS). Sin embargo, sólo unos pocos estudios han logrado demostrar que la rehabilitación podría reducir la discapacidad tras la infección por COVID-19.

Describir la respuesta del paciente PACS a un programa de rehabilitación personalizado y evaluar su tiempo de recuperación, en términos de calidad de vida y funcionalidad.

Estudio observacional de cohorte prospectivo de pacientes que habían tenido previamente COVID-19, que vivían en su domicilio y presentaban síntomas (puntuación de disnea mMRC ≥2 y puntuación de fragilidad SPPB ≥3). Ofrecemos 3 tipos de programas de rehabilitación, además de entrenamiento específico de la musculatura respiratoria: ambulatorio, domiciliario o autoadministrado. Evaluamos la fuerza muscular, la fuerza de los músculos respiratorios y la calidad de vida relacionada con la salud (CVRS) a los 3, 6 y 9 meses desde la consulta inicial.

Registramos datos de 178 pacientes con PACS, de los cuales 101 evaluados al inicio fueron admitidos en un programa de rehabilitación, (41,6%) en casa, (27,7%) como pacientes ambulatorios y (24,8%) como autoadministrados. Se prescribió entrenamiento de los músculos inspiratorios (IMT) a 56 pacientes, 50 de los cuales fueron incluidos en el programa de rehabilitación y 6 (5,9%) pacientes recibieron solo IMT. La mejora fue mayor a los 3 meses para la fuerza de la Escala modificada del Consejo de Investigación Médica (mMRC) (p=0,001), la Batería de rendimiento físico corto (SPPB) (p<0,0001), el agarre manual (p=0,017), la disnea mMRC, la presión inspiratoria máxima (PImax) (p<0,0001) y la prueba de caminata de 6 minutos (6MWT) (p=0,008). La mejora persistió a los 6 meses para la fuerza de mMRC (p=0,048), SPPB (p=0,002), PImax (p<0,0001) y 6MWT (p=0,048). La distancia 6MWT aumentó por debajo de la diferencia mínima clínicamente importante (72,18±60,7 m). Las puntuaciones de los componentes físicos y mentales relacionados con la CVRS solo mejoraron significativamente a los 3 meses.

La rehabilitación personalizada para pacientes con PACS mejoró los parámetros musculares, la disnea y la capacidad de ejercicio, pero no la CVRS, a los 6 meses. La mejora de la fuerza de los músculos respiratorios se relacionó con una mejora de la disnea y la capacidad de ejercicio.

Abstract
--------

**Introduction:** Several guidelines have proposed specific rehabilitation programs to treat symptoms/sequelae in patients with post-acute COVID syndrome (PACS). Only a few studies show that rehabilitation reduce disability after COVID-19.

**Objective:** To describe the response to a personalized rehabilitation program in terms of exercise tolerance, muscle function, dyspnea, quality of life, and frailty state in a cohort of patients with PACS.

**Methods:** Observational prospective cohort study of community-dwelling patients. The rehabilitation program included three modalities (outpatient, home-based, and self-administered), optionally supplemented with respiratory muscle training.

**Study variables:** 6-minute walking distance (6MWD), inspiratory muscle strength (PI max), dyspnea, muscle strength (estimated with the Medical Research Council scale and handgrip), health-related quality of life (HRQoL), and frailty estimated with the Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB) and at 3- and 6-months following baseline.

**Results:** Of 178 patients with PACS, 101 (56.7%) were enrolled in a rehabilitation program: 41.6% home-based, 27.7% outpatient, and 24.8% self-administered. Inspiratory muscle training (IMT) was provided to 56 patients, with 50 included in the rehabilitation program and 6 (5.9%) receiving IMT alone. Significant improvements were observed at 3 months in mMRC strength (p=0.001), SPPB (p<0.0001), handgrip strength (p=0.017), mMRC dyspnea, PI max (p<0.0001), and 6-MWD (p=0.008). Improvements persisted at 6 months for mMRC strength (p=0.048), SPPB (p=0.002), PI max (p<0.0001), and 6MWD (p=0.048). HRQoL mental and physical component scores improved only at 3 months.

**Conclusions:** Personalized rehabilitation for patients with PACS improved muscle parameters, dyspnoea, and exercise capacity, but not HRQoL, up to 6 months. Improvements in respiratory muscle strength were associated with reductions in dyspnoea and increased exercise capacity.

**Keywords:** Entrenamiento de los músculos respiratorios; Home-based rehabilitation; Outpatient rehabilitation; Post-acute COVID syndrome; Rehabilitación ambulatoria; Rehabilitación domiciliaria; Respiratory muscle training; Síndrome de Covid posagudo.

Observational prospective cohort study of community-dwelling patients. The rehabilitation program included three modalities (outpatient, home-based, and self-administered), optionally supplemented with respiratory muscle training. Study variables: 6-minute walking distance (6MWD), inspiratory muscle strength (PImax), dyspnea, muscle strength (estimated with the Medical Research Council scale and handgrip), health-related quality of life (HRQoL), and frailty estimated with the Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB) and at 3- and 6-months following baseline.

Of 178 patients with PACS, 101 (56.7%) were enrolled in a rehabilitation program: 41.6% home-based, 27.7% outpatient, and 24.8% self-administered. Inspiratory muscle training (IMT) was provided to 56 patients, with 50 included in the rehabilitation program and 6 (5.9%) receiving IMT alone. Significant improvements were observed at 3 months in mMRC strength (p=0.001), SPPB (p<0.0001), handgrip strength (p=0.017), mMRC dyspnea, PImax (p<0.0001), and 6-MWD (p=0.008). Improvements persisted at 6 months for mMRC strength (p=0.048), SPPB (p=0.002), PImax (p<0.0001), and 6MWD (p=0.048). HRQoL mental and physical component scores improved only at 3 months.

Personalized rehabilitation for patients with PACS improved muscle parameters, dyspnoea, and exercise capacity, but not HRQoL, up to 6 months. Improvements in respiratory muscle strength were associated with reductions in dyspnoea and increased exercise capacity.

Varias guías han propuesto programas de rehabilitación específicos y multidimensionales para tratar síntomas y secuelas en pacientes con síndrome COVID postagudo (PACS). Sin embargo, sólo unos pocos estudios han logrado demostrar que la rehabilitación podría reducir la discapacidad tras la infección por COVID-19.

Describir la respuesta del paciente PACS a un programa de rehabilitación personalizado y evaluar su tiempo de recuperación, en términos de calidad de vida y funcionalidad.

Estudio observacional de cohorte prospectivo de pacientes que habían tenido previamente COVID-19, que vivían en su domicilio y presentaban síntomas (puntuación de disnea mMRC ≥2 y puntuación de fragilidad SPPB ≥3). Ofrecemos 3 tipos de programas de rehabilitación, además de entrenamiento específico de la musculatura respiratoria: ambulatorio, domiciliario o autoadministrado. Evaluamos la fuerza muscular, la fuerza de los músculos respiratorios y la calidad de vida relacionada con la salud (CVRS) a los 3, 6 y 9 meses desde la consulta inicial.

Registramos datos de 178 pacientes con PACS, de los cuales 101 evaluados al inicio fueron admitidos en un programa de rehabilitación, (41,6%) en casa, (27,7%) como pacientes ambulatorios y (24,8%) como autoadministrados. Se prescribió entrenamiento de los músculos inspiratorios (IMT) a 56 pacientes, 50 de los cuales fueron incluidos en el programa de rehabilitación y 6 (5,9%) pacientes recibieron solo IMT. La mejora fue mayor a los 3 meses para la fuerza de la Escala modificada del Consejo de Investigación Médica (mMRC) (p=0,001), la Batería de rendimiento físico corto (SPPB) (p<0,0001), el agarre manual (p=0,017), la disnea mMRC, la presión inspiratoria máxima (PImax) (p<0,0001) y la prueba de caminata de 6 minutos (6MWT) (p=0,008). La mejora persistió a los 6 meses para la fuerza de mMRC (p=0,048), SPPB (p=0,002), PImax (p<0,0001) y 6MWT (p=0,048). La distancia 6MWT aumentó por debajo de la diferencia mínima clínicamente importante (72,18±60,7 m). Las puntuaciones de los componentes físicos y mentales relacionados con la CVRS solo mejoraron significativamente a los 3 meses.

La rehabilitación personalizada para pacientes con PACS mejoró los parámetros musculares, la disnea y la capacidad de ejercicio, pero no la CVRS, a los 6 meses. La mejora de la fuerza de los músculos respiratorios se relacionó con una mejora de la disnea y la capacidad de ejercicio.

Since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, many patients recovering from the acute phase of the disease have experienced persistent pulmonary and extrapulmonary symptoms and limitations. This clinical entity is widely known as post-acute COVID-19 syndrome (PACS) within our clinical practice.[1](#bib0150)

A previous article by our group characterized COVID-19 infection sequelae in three patient groups: intensive care unit (ICU)-treated, ward-treated, and non-hospitalized.[2](#bib0155) In that study, approximately 55% of patients reported persistent post-infection symptoms, primarily dyspnoea, fatigue, anxiety, depression, and limitations due to muscle weakness and frailty, resulting in moderate dependence for 3–9 months.[2](#bib0155) Muscle parameters were most affected, particularly in ICU-treated patients, while non-hospitalized patients exhibited poorer scores in anxiety, depression, and health-related quality of life (HRQoL). Furthermore, respiratory muscle weakness was observed in many patients who had not required mechanical ventilation.

Various guidelines have recommended tailored, individualized, and multidimensional rehabilitation programs for patients with PACS.[3–5](#bib0160) A recent statement of the European Respiratory Society highlighted the potential of rehabilitation to mitigate disability after COVID-19 infection.[6](#bib0175) Literature reviews indicate that approximately 45% of COVID-19 patients may require a multidisciplinary evaluation and some form of rehabilitation due to persistent symptoms.[7](#bib0180) While most rehabilitation studies have focused on inpatient or outpatient programs, research on home-based rehabilitation is limited. We believe it is important to adapt rehabilitation programs to individual patient needs and the most suitable environment, depending on clinical involvement and patient mobility.

In June 2020, our hospital created a multidisciplinary post-COVID rehabilitation unit (MPCR), staffed by a team of rehabilitation physicians, pulmonologists, physiotherapists, occupational therapists, and speech therapists. The multidisciplinary team assesses each patient to determine their rehabilitation needs and the most suitable environment for the delivery of care.

Our hypothesis posited that an individualized rehabilitation program would expedite functional recovery in patients with PACS. Consequently, the aim for this study was to describe the response to a personalized rehabilitation program in terms of exercise tolerance, muscle function, dyspnea, quality of life, and frailty state in a cohort of patients with PACS.

This prospective observational cohort study included patients with PACS, evaluated at our post-COVID rehabilitation consultation (MPCR). Conducted from June 2020 to June 2021, patients were referred from primary care (GP), the pneumology outpatient department, or at hospital discharge. The initial assessment occurred at 3 months post-infection in most cases, though some patients were referred later due to persistent symptoms.[2](#bib0155)

Patients were included if they: (1) had a prior COVID-19 infection confirmed by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test; (2) were community-dwelling; (3) had a negative PCR or immunoglobulin M result at the baseline evaluation or ≥28 days post-diagnosis; and (4) presented with symptoms defined as modified Medical Research Council (mMRC) scale dyspnea score ≥2 and Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB) frailty score ≥3. Patients were excluded if they: (1) had active COVID-19 infection; (2) history of cognitive impairment; and (3) resided outside the hospital's catchment area.

The MPCR offers three types of rehabilitation programs:

* (1)

  Outpatient rehabilitation: Outpatient rehabilitation, conducted at the Rehabilitation Department with two weekly 1-h sessions, was provided to patients with persistent dyspnea (mMRC ≥3), mild-to-moderate dependency (SPPB ≤10 points or 6-minute walking distance [6MWD] <350m), neurological disorders, and no transportation difficulties. Patients underwent physiotherapy, including aerobic, muscles strength and tone exercises, cycle ergometer training, and balance and coordination exercises. Occupational therapists assessed patients for training in energy conservation techniques, transfers, manipulation skills, gait quality, activities of daily living, and cognition. Patients received weekly telemonitoring by therapists to ensure adherence to the program guidelines until completion.
* (2)

  Home rehabilitation: Home rehabilitation was indicated for patients with moderate-to-severe dependency (Barthel Index [BI] <60; SPPB ≤9 or 6MWD ≤250m), with oxygen requirements or transportation difficulties. A physiotherapist visited patients’ homes twice weekly to train and supervise exercises tailored to individual needs. Patients were advised to perform daily 20–30min sessions, with adjustments based on clinical progress. The program included respiratory therapy (respiratory muscle training and breathing control), muscle resistance training and strengthening techniques (upper and lower limb exercises, standing, sitting and lying exercises), balance, coordination, proprioception, and stretching techniques.
* (3)

  Self-administered rehabilitation: Self-administered rehabilitation, aimed at patients with mild dependency (BI >95, SPPB >10, or 6MWD >350m), with no oxygen requirements, followed the Vivifrail frailty prevention program guidelines. They were trained during the baseline visit and the follow-up visits, how to perform the scheduled exercises.

Self-administered rehabilitation: Self-administered rehabilitation, aimed at patients with mild dependency (BI >95, SPPB >10, or 6MWD >350m), with no oxygen requirements, followed the Vivifrail frailty prevention program guidelines. In addition to this, patients conducted inspiratory muscle training (IMT). The Philips Respironics Threshold IMT® device was provided to patients with PImax ≤80% of the predicted value, regardless of their assigned rehabilitation program. Patients began with an inspiratory pressure of 30% of their measured PImax, which was progressively increased as tolerated. Patients were instructed to complete two daily 15-min sessions at home. Each session consisted of 10 repetitions with 30-s rest intervals, totaling 10–15min of exercise.

The study variables assessed included:

* -

  Exercise capacity according to the 6-minute walk test (6MWT); minimum clinically important difference (MCID) for the 6MWT, defined in 26±2m.[8,9](#bib0185)
* -

  Respiratory muscle strength measured by maximal inspiratory pressure (PImax) using the Pocket Spiro MPM-100.[10](#bib0195)
* -

  Dyspnea in daily activities according to the mMRC.[11](#bib0200)
* -

  Peripheral muscle strength according to the mMRC.[12](#bib0205)
* -

  Hand grip strength using the Jamar hydraulic hand dynamometer.[13](#bib0210)
* -

  HRQoL according to the 36-Item Short Form Survey (SF-36).[14](#bib0215)
* -

  Frailty estimated with the SPPB.[15](#bib0220)

Clinical data for all variables were documented in the patient's medical record at baseline and at 3, 6, and 9 months after the first MPCR visit ([Fig. 1](#fig0005)). During each follow-up evaluation, patients who showed significant improvement (achieving normal values in BI and SPPB, or surpassing the MCDI for 6MWT) were considered to have completed their rehabilitation program and were discharged.

This study adhered to ethical standards and recommendations for research involving human beings, including the Good Clinical Practice guidelines, the Code of Ethics, and the Declaration of Helsinki. Data processing complied with Spanish legislation and the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) of the European Union 2016/679 of April 27, 2016. The study protocol and informed consent were reviewed and approved by the local Ethics Committee (IIBSP-COV-2020-155). All participants provided written and verbal informed consent after receiving comprehensive information about the study procedures. Data were collected and recorded specifically for this study. The study was registered on ClinicalTrials.gov ([NCT04852718](ctgov:NCT04852718)).

Changes overtime in quantitative variables were analyzed using analysis of variance (ANOVA), and results were presented as means with corresponding standard deviations (SD) for the three rehabilitation groups (outpatient, home-based, and self-administered). Given the absence of significant skewness in the distribution of quantitative variables, the assumption of normality was deemed appropriate, and post-hoc Scheffé tests were employed. Categorical variables were analyzed using the Chi-square test, with results reported as absolute frequencies and percentages. The probability of a type I error was set at 5%. All statistical analyses were performed using IBM SPSS Statistics (version 26.0).

Between June 2020 and June 2021, data were collected for 178 patients (58.9% men, mean age 59.6 years). Among these patients, 151 had required hospitalization due to COVID-19 (91 requiring ICU care, 60 conventional ward hospitalization), while 27 were managed as out-patients. The average interval from hospital discharge to the initial consultation at the MPCR was 109.7±79.6 days. Notably, patients admitted during the first wave experienced a significantly longer time to their first MPCR visit than those admitted during the third wave. At the time of initial assessment, 77 patients were deemed ineligible for rehabilitation due to not meeting the predefined inclusion criteria, notwithstanding the presence of reported symptoms. Of the remaining 101 patients, 42 (41.6%) were allocated to home-based rehabilitation, 28 (27.7%) to outpatient rehabilitation, and 25 (24.8%) to a self-administered rehabilitation program. Six patients (5.9%) received only inspiratory muscle training (IMT), and 50 patients received IMT as an adjunct to their respective rehabilitation program. Overall, 56 patients participated in IMT.

From the 101 patients who started the MPCR program, 11 patients ceased because of subjective improvement (10 cases) and one patient died. Of the 90 patients assessed at the 3-month follow-up, 60 were discharged. At the 6-month evaluation, 30 patients were assessed, resulting in 17 discharges. The 9-month follow-up was attended by only 13 patients, rendering statistical analyses unreliable due to the limited sample size ([Fig. 1](#fig0005)).

Significant improvements were observed at 3 months in exercise tolerance, respiratory and peripheral muscle strength, and frailty, as detailed in [Table 2](#tbl0010). Concerning HRQoL, a significant improvement was noted in the SF-36 physical component, but not the mental component, at 3 months. The change in the 6MWD surpassed the MCID (72.2±60.7m). At the 6-month follow-up, significant improvements persisted in the 6MWD, peripheral muscle strength as measured by mMRC, and SPPB scores.

Our results reveal that the MPCR program was able to discharge approximately 50% of the patients in each follow-up, attributed to improvements in muscle parameters and symptoms alleviation. Despite a significant improvement in the physical component of HRQoL at 3 months, scores remained consistently low through discharge. In agreement with literature suggesting that approximately 45% of COVID-19 patients require multidisciplinary evaluation and rehabilitation interventions,[7](#bib0180) our study identified 56.7% of patients as suitable candidates for rehabilitation.

Distinct from the predominance of inpatient rehabilitation studies, our investigation focused on outpatient and home-based rehabilitation programs. Patients participating in our program experienced enhancements in muscle parameters and physical performance, with significant gains observed at the 3-month follow-up. While the SF-36 physical component demonstrated improvement, the mental component did not. Our findings are consistent with reports of persistent anxiety/depression in 10–40% of patients.[18–25](#bib0235) Furthermore, evidence from two reviews suggests that rehabilitation yields greater benefits than natural recovery.[21,22](#bib0250)

At 3 months, the improvement in the 6MWD observed in our sample surpassed the MCID, thereby validating the findings of Gloeckl et al.[17](#bib0230) Physical HRQoL improved, though remained below normative standards. However, the SF-36 mental component did not improved, contrasting with the findings of Maniscalco et al.,[16](#bib0225) possibly due to their program's inclusion of emotional support, not incorporated in ours. This suggests that the implementation of psychological support could offer substantial benefits.

Consistent with our prior research,[2](#bib0155) 56 patients in this cohort presented with reduced PImax values, indicative of inspiratory muscle weakness, notably in those who did not undergo invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV). Findings reported by Farr et al.[24](#bib0265) indicate that diaphragmatic contractile capacity is diminished in COVID-19 patients post-IMV compared to non-COVID-19 patients. Consequently, our observation suggests a potential role for viral infection in the development of inspiratory muscle weakness, independent of IMV. We observed that respiratory muscle training led to improvements in PImax, accompanied by a reduction in mMRC-measured dyspnea and an increase in 6MWT distance, particularly after 3 months of rehabilitation. These findings support the hypothesis that respiratory muscle weakness is a contributing factor to dyspnea on exertion, as corroborated by other investigations into respiratory muscle training as a rehabilitation strategy for COVID-19.[25,26](#bib0270) Research indicates that respiratory muscle weakness is a partial determinant of dyspnea. Langer et al.[27](#bib0280) posit that respiratory muscle hypertrophy following IMT may enhance muscle strength, and Charususin et al.[28](#bib0285) suggest that it mitigates neural respiratory drive and enhances breathing patterns.

A significant outcome, aligning with the finding of Huang et al.,[29](#bib0290) was the substantial discharge rate observed in our rehabilitation program. Specifically, only 13 of 101 patients persisted with ongoing limitations at 9-month follow-up, and merely 2 patients required continued treatment beyond the study's conclusion.

The primary strength of our study is the demonstration that rehabilitation alleviates symptoms and enhance exercise and muscle function in patients with PACS. In addition, our data suggest that respiratory muscle weakness is linked, at least clinically, to dyspnea and decreased exercise capacity. The main limitations of our study were the time elapsed between the end of the acute illness and the first MPCR visit, the fact that only altered parameters were evaluated at each follow-up, and that the sample was too small to draw conclusions at 9-month follow-up. Despite having a hundred cases, with three comparison groups not very balanced, the power can be considered fair.

Personalized rehabilitation improves muscle parameters, dyspnea, and exercise capacity and HRQoL in PACS patients at 3 months. These improvements were maintained at 6 months, except for HRQoL. Improved respiratory muscle strength was associated with reduced dyspnea and increased exercise capacity. This highlights the importance of assessing respiratory muscle strength and incorporating respiratory muscle training into rehabilitation programs when respiratory weakness is identified.

Utilizing telehealth and wearable devices in rehabilitation allows for real-time monitoring and adaptive exercise regimens, facilitating personalized care and improving patient engagement during recovery.

Establishing ongoing assessment protocols ensures continuous evaluation of recovery progress and adaptation of rehabilitation plans, helping to manage long-term effects and prevent relapses in health.
